Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have what you'd call an organized routine. I'd study when I felt like it or when something caught my attention, which wasn't super often. Most of my studying happened in bursts—like I'd suddenly get into it for a few hours, then not touch anything for days. I'm more of a hands-on learner, so sitting through structured study sessions never really worked for me.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely not regular. I'd go weeks barely looking at course stuff, then cram when something was due or when I randomly got motivated. Deadlines sometimes pushed me, but honestly even those didn't always get me moving. I had other things going on—extracurriculars, life stuff—so studying kind of fit in wherever it could.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or pure curiosity about something specific. Sometimes I'd get an email reminder and think, oh right, that course exists. Other times I'd log in just to download materials so I could look at them later on my own terms. It wasn't really a habit, more like sporadic check-ins.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became routine for me—it always felt like something I had to consciously decide to do. There were maybe a week or two where I logged in more regularly, but that faded fast. I think I just don't connect well with that kind of platform-based learning.
Q5: The video content was actually pretty useful when I watched it. I could pause, rewind, and really work through concepts at my own pace. I watched quite a bit of video content because I could do it while doing other things or when I was in the right headspace. Reading materials were okay too, but videos felt more engaging for my learning style.
Q6: The discussion boards—I literally never posted anything. They felt performative and I didn't see the point in posting just to post. I also ignored a lot of the smaller assignments, which I know hurt my completion rate. If something didn't seem directly connected to understanding the core material, I'd skip it.
Q7: It had to feel practical or genuinely interesting. If I could see how something connected to real understanding or would help me on the exam, I'd engage with it. Busy work or activities that felt like box-checking? I'd pass. I need to feel like my time is actually being used well.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't required, my brain categorized it as 'not my problem.' Required activities at least had some external pressure attached, even if I still didn't always do them. Optional activities would need to be really compelling for me to bother.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a ton of materials and studied them offline—PDFs, slides, whatever I could get. I'd print things out sometimes or work through problems on paper. I also used external resources like YouTube videos and other websites when the course materials weren't clicking for me.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened away from the LMS. I'd download a lecture, watch it on my tablet while commuting or at the gym, and work through concepts in my head. I'd do practice problems on paper, talk through ideas with friends. None of that shows up anywhere in the system.
Q11: All the time. I'd download everything in one session and then not log back in for weeks while I slowly worked through it offline. The LMS would show one login and one download, but I might spend ten hours with that material over the following days. It's just more convenient for me that way.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I just couldn't bring myself to care. Usually it was when I was busy with my extracurriculars or when the course content felt disconnected from anything meaningful. I'm pretty resilient so I didn't stress about it, but engagement definitely dipped when life got busy.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were the big one. I've got a full life outside of academics, and sometimes the course just wasn't the priority. I wasn't really confused or fatigued—more like I had limited bandwidth and chose to allocate it elsewhere. The LMS stuff could wait, or so I told myself.
Q14: Oh for sure. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd technically be 'engaging' but absorbing nothing. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning anything meaningful.
Q15: The assignments, definitely. I knew they were important for my grade but I kept putting them off because they felt tedious. I'd tell myself I'd do them later, then later never came. It's weird because I'm motivated in general, just not by that type of work. I prioritized exam prep instead.
Q16: Deadlines were like the only thing that sometimes got me to act, but even then not consistently. I'd see a deadline approaching and either rush something together or just accept I wasn't going to make it. They created urgency but not necessarily quality engagement from me.
Q17: Not really, to be honest. I didn't engage much with feedback because I wasn't submitting much work to get feedback on. When I did get comments on something, I'd glance at them but it didn't change my behavior much. I was more focused on understanding material for exams than on the assignment feedback loop.
Q18: The structure was pretty flexible, which worked for me in some ways but also let me drift. If there had been more required check-ins or mandatory participation, I might have engaged more consistently. But the loose structure meant I could get away with my sporadic approach.
Q19: The video content being available for download made things easier—I could engage on my terms. But the discussion requirements and all the small assignments made engagement feel like a chore. If the course had been more project-based or hands-on, I probably would have been way more into it.
Q20: I think some people might be genuinely trying to learn but struggling to produce finished work. Or they're exploring, reading, watching videos, but can't translate that into submitted assignments. It could also be anxiety about submitting imperfect work, or just not understanding what's expected.
Q21: That's literally me. I barely showed up in the LMS but I did well on exams because I was learning in my own way, offline. I'm good at understanding concepts quickly and performing under pressure. The LMS activity doesn't capture that I was actually engaging with the material, just not on their platform.
Q22: Logs can show when and how often someone clicks around, maybe what they download or watch. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of attention, whether someone actually understood anything, and all the learning that happens through conversation or independent exploration.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm genuinely curious and thinking about concepts even when I'm not officially studying. For me, engagement is internal—it's about mental investment, not platform activity.
Q24: Pretty much everything important about how I learn. The conversations with classmates, the offline studying, the way I synthesize information while doing other activities. My exam performance shows I learned the material well, but the LMS data makes me look like I wasn't trying. That's just not the full picture.
